results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born 1931) is a Hungarian-born American artist recognized as a primary pioneer of conceptual, environmental, and ecological art. Based in New York since the 1950s, she rose to international prominence in the late 1960s and 1970s. Denes is noted for her interdisciplinary approach, integrating science, mathematics, philosophy, and technology into her work, which she often refers to as "Visual Philosophy." She was an active member of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) collective, with records from 1969 and 1970 documenting her participation and equipment requests. In 1970, she featured in the landmark "Software" exhibition at the Jewish Museum, where she collaborated with computer scientists to produce works like the "Trigonal Ballet" computer animation. Her early career highlights include the 1968 site-specific work "Rice/Tree/Burial," acknowledged as one of the first ecological artworks, and the initiation of her "Pyramid Series" (1970) and "Map Projections" (1973). While she is widely famous for her 1982 installation "Wheatfield—A Confrontation," her foundational work in the late 1960s established her as a leader in systemic and technologically engaged art.

results.possibleIdentity: Neke Carson (born 1946 in Dallas, Texas) is an American performance artist, sculptor, and conceptual artist who became prominent in the New York art scene of the late 1960s and 1970s. After graduating from the Rhode Island School of Design (RISD) in 1968, he moved to New York City in 1969 and established a relationship with Andy Warhol, appearing in Warhol’s films and exhibiting his senior project, the 'Moon Man Fountain' (also known as the 'Double Bubble Bath'), at the Factory. This kinetic sculpture, which featured two people sitting in Plexiglas bubbles surrounded by water, was subsequently shown at the Museum of Contemporary Crafts and by the tech-focused Howard Wise Gallery. Carson was an active participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, specifically appearing in the landmark 1969 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' organized by E.A.T. at the Brooklyn Museum. He is widely recognized for creating 'Rectal Realism' in 1972, a technique where he painted using a brush or marker held in his rectum; his most famous work in this style is a 1972 portrait of Andy Warhol. Throughout his career, he has explored diverse media including photography (such as his 'Portraits from the Closet' series), music (as a 'Heavy Mellow' pianist), and theater. In 1979, he founded the LaRocka modeling agency and later served as the Director of Performances at the Robert Friedus Gallery and the Gershwin Hotel.

results.possibleIdentity: Sheila Berkley (1945–2007), whose name is occasionally spelled 'Barkley' or associated with 'Sheila Berkley Lehr', was an American artist active in the late 1960s and 1970s known for her large-scale public environments and interactive installations. She was a member and collaborator with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded to foster partnerships between the arts and engineering. Berkley specialized in the design of innovative public spaces, most notably 'portable playgrounds' and sculptural play equipment intended for urban environments. In 1971, she filed copyright for works such as a 'Play fountain' and 'sprinkler base,' which involved engineering by R.T. Blakely. Her work was featured in the 1977 exhibition 'Women in American Architecture: A Historic and Contemporary Perspective' at the Brooklyn Museum, where she provided a portable playground to make the exhibition accessible to mothers and children. Her contributions to social art and public playgrounds are also documented in Lucy Lippard’s 'Get the Message?: A Decade of Art for Social Change.'

results.possibleIdentity: Julius Tobias (1915–1999) was an American painter and sculptor who was active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He initially began his career as an abstract expressionist painter after studying at the Atelier Fernand Léger in Paris from 1949 to 1952. By the late 1960s, his work evolved toward Minimalism, notably with a series of wall-sized white paintings. In the 1970s, he transitioned into creating large-scale sculptural environments using materials such as concrete, steel, and wood. He is known to have signed his works as 'J. Tobias'. Tobias was associated with the intersection of art and technology through his collaboration with engineer Per Biorn in 1972; Biorn was a prominent figure in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Tobias was also a member of the Art Workers Coalition and participated in their 'Open Hearing' in 1969.
